coredata.dc:title: Experimental analysis of overcharged Li-polymer batteries
coredata.prism:publicationName: Case Studies in Chemical and Environmental Engineering
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2020 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.prism:publisher: The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.
coredata.dc:creator: Ciorba, Serena
Marracci, Mirko
Antonetti, Claudia
Martinelli, Marco
Caposciutti, Gianluca
Tellini, Bernardo
Raspolli Galletti, Anna Maria
coredata.dc:description: Since safety hazards can occur during the life of a Li-ion battery, understanding its behavior under abusive conditions is important for the development of a safe cell. In this work, overcharge tests on commercial Li-polymer cells were conducted in a climatic chamber, resulting in gas evolution. A comprehensive post-mortem analysis of the abused cells was carried out: the exhaust gases were identified by gas phase chromatography coupled with a thermal conductivity detector (micro-GC/TCD), then flammable and toxic species were detected; the cathode and anode materials were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS), while the electrolyte composition was studied by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Interestingly, the ambient temperature seemed to affect the degradation of the cell materials and hence the composition of the evolved gas.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Lithium-ion cell
Overcharge
Gas venting
Hazardous gas species

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors thank the Regione Toscana for funding the project “PRogetto TOscano per la Navigazione Eco-compatibile” (PROT-ONE) (CUP: 4421.02102014.072000099 - CUP CIPE: D48I1600022000) admitted to public contribution to be relied on PAR FAS 2007–2013 - Line of action 1.1 - Bando FAR-FAS 2014. Dr. F. Pacini and Dr. B. Tevenè are gratefully acknowledged for helpful discussions. The authors would also like to thank the Laboratori Archa srl for the EDS measurements.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Voltage and Current profile for K_20 ​cell (initially set at T ​= ​20 ​°C) during the overvoltage test.
Temperature profile for K_4, K_20, K_40 and K_50 ​cell during the overvoltage test.
Investigated pouch cells: a) K_50 before the overvoltage test and b) swelled after the test; c) after-test K_40, d) after-test K_20, e) after-test K_4 and f) reference cell.
SEM images of anodes (upper plots) and cathodes (lower plots) for reference cell (left), cell aged at 20 ​°C (center) and cell aged at 50 ​°C (right).
EDS elemental maps of anodes: upper plot reference cell; lower plot cell aged at 20 ​°C.

FT-IR spectra of the anode extracts: upper black line: reference cell; center red line: cell aged at 20 ​°C and lower blue line: cell aged at 50 ​°C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

FT-IR spectra of the cathode extracts: upper black line: reference cell; center red line: cell aged at 20 ​°C and lower blue line: cell aged at 50 ​°C. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)
Summary of GC/TCD results: volume percentages of the vent gases for the cells overcharged at different test temperatures.
Weight % of carbon, oxygen, fluorine and phosphorus in anodes. The reported values are averages calculated over different areas.
Weight % of carbon, oxygen, fluorine, manganese, cobalt and nickel in cathodes.

head.ce:title: Experimental analysis of overcharged Li-polymer batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry and Industrial Chemistry, University of Pisa, Pisa, Italy

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry and Industrial Chemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry and Industrial Chemistry University of Pisa, Pisa, Italy

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department DESTeC, University of Pisa, Pisa, Italy
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department DESTeC, University of Pisa, Pisa, Italy

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Since safety hazards can occur during the life of a Li-ion battery, understanding its behavior under abusive conditions is important for the development of a safe cell. In this work, overcharge tests on commercial Li-polymer cells were conducted in a climatic chamber, resulting in gas evolution. A comprehensive post-mortem analysis of the abused cells was carried out: the exhaust gases were identified by gas phase chromatography coupled with a thermal conductivity detector (micro-GC/TCD), then flammable and toxic species were detected; the cathode and anode materials were analyzed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS), while the electrolyte composition was studied by Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR). Interestingly, the ambient temperature seemed to affect the degradation of the cell materials and hence the composition of the evolved gas.

ce:section.ce:para: Li-ion batteries (LIBs) are widely used devices for their high power and low weight with applications that range from electronic devices to ground and maritime transport. In this regard, the PROT-ONE project (PROgetto TOscano per la Navigazione Eco-compatibile) funded by Tuscany Region aims at developing new technological solutions for hybrid/electric ferries and water buses in order to improve the cost-effectiveness trade-off of eco-friendly solutions respect to the traditional diesel one. In this contest, the study of methods for increasing the intrinsic safety of the batteries and the implementation of highest safety standards modular Li-polymer batteries represent two essential targets. To address these topics, the analysis of Li-ion cells behavior under abusive conditions can be helpful in order to focus possible strategies for improving battery safety.

An overcharge, caused for example by a defective charger or its incorrect use, can be a rare event but could lead to possible catastrophic results, causing emission of toxic/corrosive materials, up to fire and explosion of the battery.

In the literature, many studies concern abusive tests on LIBs dealing with different types of cathode, being graphite the most common material for the anode [1–3]. Nonetheless, the degradation reactions that occur during the abuse still remain unclear. However, many of those undesired processes involve the electrolyte in the cell. Common electrolytes are lithium salts, such as lithium hexafluorophosphate (LiPF6), dissolved in mixture of carbonate-based solvents, such as ethylene carbonate (EC), diethyl carbonate (DEC), dimethyl carbonate (DMC) and propylene carbonate (PC). Although liquid electrolytes ensure high ionic conductivity at room temperature and wetting of the electrode surfaces, they are flammable, volatile at high temperature and can undergo irreversible electrochemical and thermal processes. Upon cycling, non-uniform electrodeposition of lithium metal in liquid electrolytes could lead to crack formation in the protective solid-electrolyte surface (SEI) layer and generate dendritic micro-structures, resulting in increased impedance and capacity fading. Heightened metal tips could even pass through the polymeric separator between electrodes causing internal short-circuit. A promising approach that has been proposed to enable the drawbacks associated with organic solvents is the use of solid electrolytes, such as solid inorganic ones [4] and gel polymer electrolytes [5,6]. On the other hand, more research efforts are needed in order to pave the way for solid-state Li-ion batteries applications [7].

In a previous work [8] the authors presented a preliminary analysis of electrical behavior of a conventional cell overcharged at a single temperature of 50 ​°C. In this paper we significantly extend the analysis of the effect of overcharge on commercial cells simulating different surrounding conditions in which a cell can operate (i.e. different ambient temperature). The aim of this investigation is the knowledge of the environment effects on the severity of a cell failure and provide information that may help in the development of adequate battery management systems. The behavior of LIBs in harsh circumstances is well known, but yet malfunctions can occur also under normal conditions. For this reason, in the present research, electrical tests were conducted maintaining the cells within their nominal operating temperature range. An accurate identification of the gaseous products and the influence of the temperature on the gas formation as well as an attempt to understand the mechanism of gas generation were made. A post-mortem electrode surface morphology and elemental analysis as well as the electrolyte composition analysis are also discussed throughout the paper.

Kokam pouch cells, model SLPB98188216P, 30Ah nominal capacity, were used for the electrical tests. The cells under investigation represent a quite diffuse medium-high capacity device, for which an overcharge failure can lead to significant consequences due to the high energy density of the cell itself.
The cathode of this type of Kokam cell is made of lithium nickel manganese cobaltite material (NMC) and the anode is composed of carbon/graphite.

The nominal voltage of the cell is 3.6 V with a maximum charge voltage (CC-CV) of 4.2 V. The maximum continuous declared discharge current is 600 A (corresponding to about 20C, being C the nominal battery capacity, in this case 30 Ah) and maximum pulse discharge of 900 A (corresponding to about 30C) is allowed for maximum 10 seconds.

The conducted overcharge test consisted in charging the cell with a constant current (CC) I ​= ​10 A (corresponding to 1/3C) up to 4.7 V, overtaking the manufacturer maximum value of 4.2 V. During the test, the ambient temperature was kept constant and the cell temperature was monitored. In order to control the ambient temperature, the cell was placed in a precision refrigerating/warming test chamber with program control (Genviro-060LC) with temperature control that ranges from T ​= ​−70 ​°C up to T ​= ​+180 ​°C and a humidity control that ranges from 10% up to 98%. Before starting the overcharge test, the cell was trained at ambient temperature (T ​= ​20 ​°C) with several complete CC charge/discharge cycles at the constant current of I ​= ​10 A with a voltage ranging from the minimum value of 2.5 V and the maximum value of 4.2 V.

After the overvoltage test end, the cell was completely discharged up to 0 V to allow the subsequent chemical analysis. One reference cell, which did not undergo any electrical test but only the training phase, was used for comparative analysis.

During the whole tests the cell temperature was measured by two commercial platinum thermoresistances (RTD) PT100 placed on the battery case near the cathode terminal and in the center of the battery. The cell voltage was measured by a 16-bit data acquisition system directly at the cell terminals while the current was measured by a 16-bit data acquisition system through a calibrated shunt resistor. All the measurement signals (voltage, current, temperatures) were recorded by a National Instrument Labview™ based software which automatically controls all the charging/discharging phases and also implements safety controls.

Gas samples were analyzed by connecting the aged cells directly to a gas chromatograph system (GC, Agilent Technologies 3000 Micro-GC). The GC is coupled with a thermal conductivity detector (TCD) and equipped with two analytical channels: the first one is a column Molsieve 5A for the separation of helium (He), molecular hydrogen (H2), molecular oxygen (O2), molecular nitrogen (N2), methane (CH4) and carbon monoxide (CO) using argon as gas carrier. The second channel is a column PLOT U for the separation of carbon dioxide (CO2), ethylene (C2H4), ethane (C2H6), acetylene (C2H2) and propane (C3H8) using helium as gas carrier. The gases concentrations are reported as volume percentages, calculated on the basis of the total gas volume identified.

Samples for scanning electron microscopy (SEM) images and energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) spectra were extracted from cathode and anode of the investigated cells. The cells were opened in a nitrogen-filled glove box. Cathode and anode sheets were extracted from the cell stack, rinsed with dimethyl carbonate and dried under vacuum. SEM images were registered on Coxem-EM30 (Media System Lab) microscopy. EDS measurements were carried out on a FlexSEM 1000 (Hitachi) instrument. Electrolyte samples for Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) measurements were prepared by methanol extraction from electrodes. The spectra were recorded with a Spectrum-Two PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrometer.

Tests were conducted for different cells at different initial temperatures (K_4 ​cell (initially set at 4 ​°C), K_20 ​cell (test at 20 ​°C); K_40 ​cell (test at 40 ​°C) and K_50 ​cell (test at about 50 ​°C)). The set values were selected to assess the overcharge effects on the cells in likely operating conditions, without overcoming the working maximum temperature declared by the producer (Tmax ​= ​60 ​°C). The voltage and current profile during the overvoltage test for the K_20 ​cell is reported as example in Fig. 1 .

Fig. 2 shows the temperature profile acquired during all the overvoltage tests: after overcoming the nominal voltage of 4.2 V, at about 4.5 V, it is observed a rapid increase of the cells temperature, corresponding to cells swelling and formation of gases. It is worth noting that, during all the tests, the self-heating of the cells was not sufficient to exceed 60 ​°C. Hence, the temperature of the cells remains well below 80–90 ​°C, that are values generally accepted as the temperatures at which the first reactions of uncontrolled thermal runaway begin [2]. Nevertheless, exhaust gases are produced also under those mild abusive conditions, only when cut-off voltage is reached. The reference cell (only trained) did not show any external case deformation or appreciable gas evolution.
Fig. 3 displays the pouch cell K_50 before the overvoltage test and swelled after the test. Also the reference cell and aged cells K_4, K_20 and K_40 are shown in Fig. 3.
Table 1 reports the GC/TCD analysis of the vent gases from the aged cells. The results are reported as volume percentage of each analyte, calculated on the basis of the total gas volume identified.

The main observed species were above all H2 (35–69%), then CO2 (14–33%) and CO (3–12%). Short chain hydrocarbons, such as CH4 (about 3–8%), C2H4 (about 1–4%), C2H6 (about 2–4%) and C2H2 (less than 1%) were also detected.

From the obtained results, it was possible to observe that molecular hydrogen concentration decreases with the increase of the initial temperature of the electrical test (from room temperature up to about 50 ​°C), while CO and CO2 concentrations increase; also organic gases slightly increase with the enhancement of the test temperature. Since the results are reported as volume percentages, no absolute volume of each component is determined. The inverse temperature dependence of volume % for CO2 and H2 indicates that an enhanced formation of CO2 at 50 ​°C occurs, without necessarily having inhibition of H2 evolution. In general, the total gas mixture volume produced is appreciably higher at more elevated test temperatures, as shown in Fig. 3. In the case of the cell overcharged at low temperature (4 ​°C), the gas mixture composition was quite similar to the one obtained at room temperature. However, for the low temperature test, a reduced amount of gas was produced and thus the analysis might be affected by the presence of O2 and N2 from the environment. Then, other gases concentrations for this cell might be underestimated.

The gases detected in our experiments are comparable with those produced by abused lithium-ion cells in many literature studies. In general, the concentration values of the single gas species depend on the experimental parameters, such as state of charge (SOC), charge rate and temperature, and the cell type, electrolyte composition and cathode material [9–12]. On the other hand, the total amount of the produced gas is mainly affected by capacity, energy density and SOC. Indeed, the increase of those parameters lead to more severe thermal runaway [13,14]. Also the charge rate plays a role in the magnitude of gas formation: the cell can explode under high current, whereas only swells under small current [15].

With respect to the literature data, we observed lower concentrations of carbon dioxide, in favor of higher concentrations of hydrogen, especially in the case of the cell overcharged at room temperature (14% and 69% for CO2 and H2, respectively). On the contrary, the results obtained for the cell overcharged at 50 ​°C (33% and 35% for CO2 and H2, respectively) seem to be more in agreement with the values presented in previous papers [1,11,16,17].

For instance, Yuan et al. [16] investigated the behavior of a home-made cell with similar characteristics to those of the commercial batteries studied in the present work (capacity of 32 Ah and cathode NMC). They observed that under a voltage of 5.1 V, the cell reached the external temperature of 100 ​°C and broke the casing. Moreover, the authors reported that with the increase of SOC from 170 to 190%, the CO2 concentration increased from 32 to 58%, while organic gases decreased (H2 was not detectable with their instrumentation). Other authors described overcharge abuse on different types of lithium-based cell, for example Oshaki et al. [17] applied to prismatic cells 633048 (650 mAh, cathode LiCoCO2) an overcharge of 1C up to 7.5 V, reaching a temperature of 120 ​°C. They revealed CO2 (50%) with H2 (13%), CO (5%) and hydrocarbons. They also observed the gas evolution behavior of cells kept in a water bath at different temperatures: the cells overcharged at 25 ​°C and 40 ​°C evolved an amount of CO2 smaller than that of H2, while the quantity of CO2 increased when the cells were overcharged at 60 ​°C or higher temperatures.

In the literature works cited above it is highlighted that the production of CO2 is enhanced by the increase in the severity of the operating conditions, such as higher SOC and cell temperature. Such trend is in agreement with the results obtained in our study where the amount of CO2 gas produced increased with the increment of the ambient temperature. Indeed, one of the pathways of CO2 formation is the oxidation reaction of the electrolyte at the cathode that can be accelerated at the temperature above 40 ​°C [17]. In addition, an initial degradation of organic carbonates and salt, as described in section 3.5, could be favored by overcharging above 50 ​°C.

On the other hand, it has to be considered that in our experiments, the cells were subjected to moderate abusive conditions compared to literature: the applied current of 10 A corresponds to a charge rate of only 1/3 of capacity (1/3C); the overcharge was not maintained for a prolonged time and, immediately after the gas formation, the voltage was decreased in order to avoid any rupture of the external case. Thus, a state of charge of about 130% was obtained, which represents a small surplus with respect the nominal capacity of a cell.

Moreover, as shown in the temperature profiles (Fig. 2), all the cells remained under 60 ​°C during the test, which is the maximum value recommended by the manufacturer for a proper use of the devices. In this case, the self-heating of the cells during overcharging might not be sufficient to initiate the highest exothermic reactions that can occur in a heavily abused lithium-ion cell.
Therefore, we might be able to detect the incipient formation of species produced by overcharging, whose possible routes of synthesis are described in section 3.5.
The main concern about the evolved gases is that most of them are flammable (H2, CO and hydrocarbons) and could be harmful to health (CO and, at high concentration, CO2.).
Taking into consideration the importance of the issue, it would be advisable that protection solutions are adopted when the devices are utilized.

Especially when a number of cells are connected in a pack, overheating of a single cell and failure propagation to the neighboring ones need to be prevented in order to avoid high amount of hazardous gases or severe explosion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Electrode surface morphology and elemental analysis
ce:section.ce:para: After the analysis of the gases that were evolved inside the soft-pouch case, the aged cells were disassembled in an inert atmosphere glove-box and their electrodes, both anode and cathode, were characterized using ex-situ SEM and EDS. For comparison, also a reference cell (a trained cell that was not overcharged) was disassembled and studied with the same procedure.

The scanning electron microscopy allowed us to investigate the morphology of the electrode surface: SEM images recorded on graphitic carbon anodes of the reference and the cells aged at 20 ​°C and 50 ​°C are shown in Fig. 4 (upper plots).
The surface of the reference anode appears homogeneous and smooth and also in the case of the aged anodes no cracks are evident.
However, the aged anodes seem to be slightly rough, denoting that a change on the anode surface may be occurred during overcharge.

The energy dispersive X-ray spectrometry let to obtain the elemental micro-analysis of the electrode samples. Table 2 reports the EDS results for the reference and aged anodes: as expected, for all the anodes, the surface mostly consists of carbon with low weight percentages of oxygen, fluorine and phosphorus. Fluorine and phosphorus probably come from common electrolyte compounds, such as lithium hexafluorophosphate salt and additives [18], that may be remained on the electrode after sample preparation. On the other hand, compounds containing oxygen, such as inorganic lithium carbonates and alkyl lithium carbonates, are formed by reduction of the electrolyte on the anode surface during the training phase. This process, also referred to as “cell formation”, forms the SEI layer which is a protective film and prevents further deterioration of the electrolyte under normal charging conditions. On the contrary, in the case of the abused cells, we observed an increase of oxygen (from 5% of reference cell up to about 12%) and fluorine (from 13% up to about 22%), indicating that more degradation compounds may have been deposited during overcharging. The modification of the anode surface is induced by aging already at room temperature, as also shown by the EDS elemental maps in Fig. 5 . The EDS image of the reference anode sample (Fig. 5, upper plot) shows the graphite (carbon) surface partially covered by deposits containing O, F and P, but in the image of the anode aged at 20 ​°C (Fig. 5, lower plot) it seems that uncoated graphite is less visible due to a more important presence of other elements.

It has to be considered that traditional detectors in SEM and EDS techniques are not able to accurately detect elements lighter than sodium [19]. Then, any initial plating of metallic lithium on the anode could not be observed. However, lithium plating is more likely to be favored when the cell undergoes a high number of cycles charge/discharge, while in our test only one step of overcharge, followed by discharge, was applied.

In summary, both SEM measurements and EDS semi-quantitative analysis indicated that some anode surface modification result after the abuse. On the other hand, in the case of the cathode, no important differences in the morphology among reference and aged cells were evidenced (see SEM images in Fig. 4). Moreover, Table 3 reports the elemental analysis of the reference and aged cathode samples: the presence of oxygen, manganese, cobalt and nickel is in agreement with the fact that the active material is a metal oxide (NMC), while carbon and fluorine may derive from electrolyte compounds. The composition of the surface did not change after the electrical test, confirming that no degradation reactions involved the cathode material. Indeed, more drastic circumstances, especially higher temperature, would be necessary in order to induce cathode structure collapse with release of oxygen [20].

As mentioned above, common electrolyte solution in lithium-based batteries are complex mixtures of compounds, containing not only a lithium salt dissolved in one or more organic solvents, but also various additives. However, manufacturers maintain the concentration of the additives lower than 10% by weight, to avoid any negative interference with the cell performance. By means of Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy, we studied the main components of the electrolyte in the investigated cells with the aim, above all, of ascertaining modifications due to the aging.
In Fig. 6 the FT-IR spectra registered for the electrolyte extracted from the anodes of the reference cell and the cells overcharged at 20 ​°C and 50 ​°C are reported.

The spectrum of the reference anode extract (Fig. 6 – upper black line) is similar to the spectra previously published for LiPF6 in organic carbonates solvents [21,22]. Two intense peaks of the PF6 - anion, placed at 558 and 836 cm-1, are assigned to the F–P bending and F–P stretching modes, respectively. The bands in the range 1750–1810 cm-1 may be due to residual carbonate solvents. However, in the present spectrum other bands appear, one located at 1636 cm-1 attributed to the CO2 stretching mode of RCO3Li and another one at 1277 cm-1 that may be assigned to the CO stretching of R–CO3Li, pointing out the formation of lithium alkyl carbonates from the first cycles of charge/discharge. The spectrum of the extract of the anode aged at 20 ​°C (Fig. 6 – center red line) shows the peaks of the salt and also slightly increased peaks of the carbonates (located at 1636 and 1277 cm-1), indicating, as expected, that some more R–CO3Li could be originated during the aging process. The electrolyte decomposition becomes conspicuous by increasing the overcharge test temperature, indeed in the IR spectrum of the high temperature aged anode (Fig. 6 – lower blue line) the characteristic bands of the electrolyte are strongly decreased, whereas an enhancement of the band at 1634 cm-1, a band shifted from 1277 to 1327 cm-1 and a new band at 1040 cm-1 that may be assigned to C–O–C stretching mode of R–CO3Li are observed.

An IR characterization of also the cathode extracts was carried out (see Fig. 7 ). For the reference cell, the cathode extract spectrum shows similar bands to those of the anode one. As a matter of fact, the solid electrolyte interphase formed on metal oxide cathode, under normal cycling, can have a composition very similar to the one formed on carbon anode since carbonates can migrate from anode to cathode surface [20].

As it was observed in the case of the anode, also from the comparison of the cathode spectra, a significant degradation of the electrolyte at high temperature overcharge was highlighted. Indeed, for the 50 ​°C aged cathode extract, the electrolyte bands mostly disappear and a large and intense band at about 580 cm-1 is observed that may correspond to the signal of phosphate species. The latter may be due to an initial degradation of salt: lithium hexafluorophospate may form reactive phosphorous oxyfluoride that causes decarboxylation of carbonates-based solvents with release of CO2 and formation of phosphate, as will be described in section 3.5.

As said previously, during the overcharge test, all the investigated cells released gas mixtures containing H2, CO2, CO, CH4, C2H4, C2H6 and C2H2. On the basis of the results obtained with SEM and FT-IR studies, it is reasonable to say that those gaseous species were mainly formed by reactions involving the electrolyte. For completeness, Appendix A lists some of the electrochemical and thermal pathways for the electrolyte that have been proposed in previous studies and that may explain also our results, taking into consideration the moderate abusive conditions of our experiments.

As regards the formation of hydrogen, different processes have been proposed in literature, some of them occurring at high temperature, such as the reaction of the binder material, like polyvinylidene fluoride or carboxymethyl cellulose, with Li0 at above 200 ​°C [13,23] or the release of atomic hydrogen from the graphite electrode at above 110 ​°C, followed by molecular hydrogen recombination [24]. However, given the significantly lower temperatures reached in the present work (the maximum external cell temperature measured was about 60 ​°C), it is more likely that the anodic reduction of solvents is responsible of the hydrogen generation (eq. (1) - (2) in Appendix A) [21].

Regarding the formation of carbon dioxide, alkyl carbonates can decompose to CO2 in presence of trace impurities in the electrolyte, such as water (H2O), as described in eq. (3) and hydrofluoric acid (HF, decomposition product of LiPF6), as reported in eq. (4) [12,21]. Then CO2 can react with Li+ to form CO, according eq. (5) [21]. In charged cells, alkyl carbonates can also thermally decompose releasing CO2 (examples in eq. (6) [23,25].

Many authors hypothesized that the oxidation reaction of organic carbonates with O2 contained in the battery can form carbon dioxide (an example in eq. (7)) [17,23]. Nevertheless, the commercial cells investigated in this work were probably assembled in an inert atmosphere, so just a limited amount of O2 might be present in the cells at the beginning of the test and oxygen is unlikely to have been released from the cathode without exceeding 180 ​°C. Alternative reactions with CO2 evolution without oxygen involvement could be re-opening and polymerisation of EC catalysed by LiPF6 [26] and thermal decomposition of carbonate esters eq. (8) - (9) [11].

In addition, in the presence of impurities, LiPF6 may produce POF3 which reacts with the electrolyte in a decarboxylation reaction with CO2 release and phosphate formation, as described in eq. (10) - (12) [11].

It has to be considered that stronger oxidation ability and reducing ability were exhibited on cathode and anode, respectively, under overcharge conditions than normal conditions [20]. In accordance with this fact, possible routes leading to the formation of CO2 are electrolyte oxidation at the cathode [17], ethylene carbonate reduction at the carbon surface and linear carbonate solvents decomposition in presence of lithium methoxide coming from the reduction of DMC [23].

An electrochemical origin is the most probable also for hydrocarbons and carbon monoxide, being CH4, C2H4, C2H6 and CO produced from reduction reactions of organic carbonate-based solvents on the anode surface (eq. (13) - (21)), while C2H2 derives from oxidation of C2H4 on the cathode surface (eq. (22)) [12].

In the present paper the overcharge test on commercial pouch cells was described and the results of post-mortem analysis for the abused devices were discussed. The test was conducted on Kokam cells by applying an overvoltage of 4.7 V at different operating temperatures under 60 ​°C so that to avoid abnormal conditions of abuse.

The outcome of overcharge varied with the test conditions: by means of gas chromatography, hydrogen was identified as the main component of the gas mixture emitted by the cells thermostated at 4 and 20 ​°C, while the CO2 concentration increased with the temperature of the electrical test. Also CO and hydrocarbons were detected, following a behavior similar to the CO2 trend.

Moreover, the SEM/EDS measurements of the electrodes evidenced appreciable changes on the anode surface after overcharge and the FT-IR characterization of the electrolyte indicated that the aging effect, in particular the electrolyte degradation, was enhanced by the increase of the set temperature. Hence, the environment conditions in which a cell operates can affect the gravity of a failure.

The results of such analysis with the detection of gas mixtures rich in hydrogen represent, in our opinion, an important piece of information from a safety point of view and can be helpful for developing protection systems aimed at preventing possible hazardous conditions in the use of Li-based cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors thank the Regione Toscana for funding the project “PRogetto TOscano per la Navigazione Eco-compatibile” (PROT-ONE) (CUP: 4421.02102014.072000099 - CUP CIPE: D48I1600022000) admitted to public contribution to be relied on PAR FAS 2007–2013 - Line of action 1.1 - Bando FAR-FAS 2014. Dr. F. Pacini and Dr. B. Tevenè are gratefully acknowledged for helpful discussions.
The authors would also like to thank the Laboratori Archa srl for the EDS measurements.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the Supplementary data to this article:
Multimedia component 1 Multimedia component 1

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix B
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cscee.2020.100012.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Main reactive pathways involving electrolyte and leading to the formation of gaseous species.
(1) DEC + 2e − + 2Li + → LiCH 2 CH 2 CO 3 CH 2 CH 2 Li ↓ ​+ ​H2 ↑
(2) EC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + → LiCH 2 CH 2 OCO 2 Li ↓ ​+ ​1/2H2
(3) 2RCO 3 Li ​+ ​H2O → Li 2 CO 3 ↓+ 2ROH ​+ ​CO2 ↑
(4) RCO 3 Li ​+ ​HF → LiF ​+ ​ROH ​+ ​CO2 ↑
(5) CO2 ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + → Li 2 CO 3 ↓+ CO ↑
(6) (CH2OCO2Li)2 → Li 2 CO 3 ↓+ CH4 ↑ ​+ ​CO2 ↑+ 1/2O2 ↑
(7) DEC ​+ ​6O2 → 5H2O ​+ ​5CO2 ↑
(8) DMC → CH3OCH3 ​+ ​CO2 ↑
(9) EC → C2H4O ​+ ​CO2 ↑
(10) LiPF6 → LiF ​+ ​PF5
(11) PF5 ​+ ​ROH → HF ​+ ​RF ​+ ​POF3
(12) POF3 ​+ ​solvent → phosphate ​+ ​CO2 ↑
(13) DMC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + ​+ ​H2 → Li 2 CO 3 ↓+ CH4 ↑
(14) DMC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + ​+ ​H2 → CH 3 OLi ↓+ CH4 ↑
(15) EC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + → Li 2 CO 3 ↓+ C2H4 ↑
(16) 2EC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + → (CH 2 OCO2Li)2 ↓+ C2H4 ↑
(17) DEC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + ​+ ​H2 → Li 2 CO 3 ↓+ 2C2H6 ↑
(18) DEC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + ​+ ​H2 → CH 3 CH 2 CH 2 CO 2 Li ↓ ​+ ​C2H6 ↑
(19) DMC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + → CH 3 OLi ↓+ CO ↑
(20) EC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + → (CH 2 OLi)2 ↓+ CO ↑
(21) DEC ​+ ​2e − ​+ ​2Li + → CH 3 CH 2 OLi ↓+ CO ↑
(22) C2H4 → C2H2 +2H + + 2e −

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Identification and quantification of gases emitted during abuse tests by overcharge of a commercial Li-ion battery

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y. Fernandes, A. Bry, S. De Persis, Identification and quantification of gases emitted during abuse tests by overcharge of a commercial Li-ion battery, J. Power Sources 389 (2018) 106-119.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Toxic gas emissions from damaged lithium ion batteries, analysis and safety enhancement solution

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A. Nedjalkov, J. Meyer, M. Kohring, A. Doering, M. Angelmahr, S. Dahle, A. Sander, A. Fischer, W. Schade, Toxic gas emissions from damaged lithium ion batteries, analysis and safety enhancement solution, Batteries 2 (2016) 5-14.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thermal runaway and fire behaviors of large-scale lithium ion batteries with different heating methods

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Z. Wang, H. Yang, Y. Li, G. Wang, J. Wang, Thermal runaway and fire behaviors of large-scale lithium ion batteries with different heating methods, J. Hazard. Mater. 379 (2019) 120730-120737.

Shao, Water-proof, electrolyte-nonvolatile, and flexible Li-Air batteries via O2-Permeable silica-aerogel-reinforced polydimethylsiloxane external membranes, Energy Stor. Mater. 27 (2020) 297-306.
